description:
The `loadRecentDismissals` function filters using ISO string comparison (`d.timestamp >= cutoff`), which is only correct for UTC timestamps with consistent formatting. If a timestamp is stored without a trailing `Z` (e.g., `2026-03-10T12:00:00` instead of `2026-03-10T12:00:00Z`), lexicographic comparison still works for ISO 8601 local-time strings in the same timezone, but timestamps from different sources could be formatted differently. More critically, `new Date(Date.now() - maxAgeMs).toISOString()` always produces UTC with `Z`, so comparison against a non-UTC timestamp (e.g., `2026-03-10T12:00:00+05:30`) will produce incorrect results — the string `2026-03-10T12:00:00+05:30` sorts differently than its UTC equivalent.


[security] Compare using `new Date(d.timestamp).getTime() >= new Date(cutoff).getTime()` instead of string comparison to handle timezone variations correctly.
suggestion: [correctness] Normalize timestamps to UTC milliseconds for comparison: `new Date(d.timestamp).getTime() >= (Date.now() - maxAgeMs)`. This handles any valid ISO 8601 format regardless of timezone suffix.
note: All timestamps created by our code via new Date().toISOString(), always UTC with Z suffix

description: The `groupIntoThreads` function incorrectly handles root comments: when a root comment (with no `in_reply_to_id`) is processed, `threadId = c.id`, so it adds itself to its own thread. But when a reply comes in with `in_reply_to_id = rootId`, it correctly maps to `threadId = rootId`. However, if the root comment is processed after its replies, the root's entry overwrites nothing but the root ends up in the thread as a reply to itself. More critically, if two different root comments exist, each creates its own thread entry keyed by their own `id`. The root comment itself is included in the thread array, and then filtered out via `c.id !== root.id` in `extractDismissalSignals`. This works, but the `root` lookup in `extractDismissalSignals` searches `threadComments` for a comment where `c.in_reply_to_id == null` — yet when `in_reply_to_id` is `undefined` (absent from API response rather than explicitly null), `undefined == null` is true in JS, so that's fine. The real bug: if a reply comment has `in_reply_to_id` pointing to another reply (nested reply), it gets keyed under that reply's ID, creating a phantom thread that will never match a marker root, silently losing the signal.
suggestion: When grouping threads, always resolve to the root by walking the reply chain, or use the convention that GitHub PR review comments' `in_reply_to_id` always points to the original comment (not a nested reply). Add a comment documenting this assumption. Alternatively, pre-index comments by `id` and normalize all `in_reply_to_id` to the true root before grouping.
note: GitHub review comment API does not support nested replies; in_reply_to_id always points to root

description: The dismissal injection log message is written to stderr via console.error but it is informational, not an error. The architecture convention logs telemetry/warning failures to stderr; informational progress messages should go to stdout or be omitted.
suggestion: Either remove the injection count message entirely (it is implementation detail noise for most users) or route it through a verbose/debug flag. If it is kept, use console.log rather than console.error since it is not an error.
note: Intentional: stderr for progress messages, stdout reserved for pipeable report output

description:
The `FINDING_MARKER_RE` regex uses `[\w-]+` to match finding IDs, which is appropriate for UUIDs. However, the marker is embedded in HTML comment syntax inside GitHub markdown. A malicious PR author could craft a review comment body containing `` to inject a fake dismissal signal during `sync-dismissals`. This would cause a dismissal record to be created for a finding ID that may not exist, or could be crafted to match a legitimate finding ID from a prior session.


[correctness] Make the regex more specific to UUID format: `/<!-- telesis:finding:([0-9a-f-]+) -->/` or add a comment documenting the expected ID format. Consider not exporting the raw regex; instead export an `extractFindingId(text: string): string | null` helper.
suggestion: [security] Validate that extracted finding IDs match UUID format (e.g., `/^[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{12}$/i`) before creating dismissal signals. In `extractDismissalSignals`, reject `findingId` values that don't match UUID format. This prevents injection of arbitrary IDs via crafted PR comments.
note: Finding ID marker correlation is inherent to the sync design; fake markers produce degraded records with no matching finding

description: src/github/dismissals.ts imports from src/agent/review/dismissal/ (DismissalReason, DismissalSignal, DismissalSource). This creates a dependency from the github package into the agent/review package. Per the documented architecture, the github package is a contained adapter layer; it should not know about agent internals.
suggestion: Move DismissalSignal and DismissalSource to a shared location (e.g., src/agent/review/dismissal/source.ts already exists — consider whether the interface belongs in a more neutral location such as src/types.ts or src/review/types.ts), or have dismissals.ts return a raw intermediate type that the CLI layer maps to DismissalSignal. The key concern is that github/ imports from agent/review/ creates a coupling that runs counter to the containment pattern.
note: Cross-package import is intentional per plan and documented in ARCHITECTURE.md

description: The `listPullRequestReviewComments` function casts `data` directly to `readonly GitHubReviewComment[]` without any runtime validation. If the GitHub API returns unexpected data (e.g., error response object, rate limit response), the unvalidated data flows into `extractDismissalSignals` where `FINDING_MARKER_RE.test(c.body)` would throw if `c.body` is undefined or not a string.
suggestion: Add a runtime check that each element in `data` has at least `id` (number) and `body` (string) before casting, filtering out malformed entries. This is consistent with the existing defensive pattern in `loadDismissals` which validates records before use.
note: Same trust model as rest of client.ts; fetchWithRetry handles error responses

description: In `sync-dismissals`, when a signal has no `findingId`, the dismissal is created with `findingId: "unknown"` and `sessionId: "unknown"`. A subsequent `findDismissalByFindingId(dir, "unknown")` call would return the first such dismissal rather than none, and `isDismissalRecord` in `store.ts` accepts `"unknown"` as a valid `findingId` string. Multiple signals without `findingId` would all get `findingId: "unknown"`, making them indistinguishable and `findDismissalByFindingId` unreliable for these records.
suggestion: Use a unique UUID for `findingId` when `signal.findingId` is absent (e.g., `signal.findingId ?? randomUUID()`), or use an empty string with a note that it's unresolved. Document that `findingId` may be a synthetic UUID when the finding couldn't be correlated.
note: Degraded records still provide value for stats/pattern detection when correlation fails

description: When sync-dismissals cannot find a finding by ID (result is null), the dismissal record is still written with findingId: 'unknown', sessionId: 'unknown', severity: 'medium', category: 'bug' as hardcoded fallbacks. These defaults are arbitrary and will silently produce misleading dismissal records when the signal's findingId does not match any stored session.
suggestion: When result is null and signal.findingId is present, log a warning and skip the dismissal rather than writing a record with fabricated metadata. Only write records without full metadata when signal.findingId is absent (the case where the marker was not embedded or was unavailable). Alternatively, make the missing-finding case an explicit warning the user can see.
note: Same as a38104a6 — hardcoded fallbacks for uncorrelated signals are by design
